comment_content: This movie is a true account of the kind of society you have in the UK. Hats off and a big salute to the director and the beautiful script
reply_content:
@sami2503 easy access to the internet... plus atomized society... plus the erosion of traditional social structures and cultural values... and so on.
obviously the UK doesn't have a monopoly for all of that in a literal sense, as in being the only country in the world where these things happen.
however, they tend to be worse in Western Europe than in Central Europe right now, as the latter has stricter school environment, lower rates of teenage pregnancies, less broken families (e.g. in Poland - where I'm from, while I've also lived in the UK for several years - only about 1 in 10 of households are single-parent families, whereas in the UK it's roughly 1 in 4), etc.
it would be absurd to assume that all these differences have no substantial social impact.
you can find the statistics easily...
and yes, banning phones in schools is not a real solution, it only suppresses a symptom.

The Netflix show Addolences is yet another example of how far Western culture has drifted from reality, responsibility, and reason. Instead of portraying meaningful growth or authentic struggles, it glamorizes dysfunctional behaviour and disoriented values. This is a clear symptom of a civilization that's losing its grounding in tradition, accountability, and societal cohesion.

What’s most troubling is how these kinds of fictional attitudes—over-the-top emotional drama, hyper-individualism, and disregard for any sense of cultural continuity—are increasingly becoming normalized, not just on screen but in real life, particularly in the West. It’s hard to imagine such storylines being taken seriously in many non-Western societies, where family, honor, and discipline still hold weight. Instead, Addolences pushes a narrative that seems almost alien to much of the rest of the world—one where personal gratification trumps collective well-being, and identity is built on rebellion rather than roots.

Western media, once a leader in storytelling and innovation, now often seems trapped in a feedback loop of self-deprecation, nihilism, and moral ambiguity. If Addolences is any reflection of what’s considered “relatable” or “real” in modern Western youth culture, it’s a grim forecast for the future of that society.

As someone who left school in 2021, I don't think this show is as good as you guys are saying. This is something clearly made by University-educated, middle class Gen X trying to explain an crisis in Gen Z Working Class boys. 

People like Andrew Tate didn't create a problem, they merely capitalized on an already existing one. Why is it always social media that is blamed? It's all I've ever heard in school, social media this, social media that. I didn't even have any social media until I left school, yet I still had a lot of problems. 

For a start, there is a lack of places specifically for kids and that kids can afford anymore. No youth clubs or youth centres for kids to go and socialize at after school, many schools don't have a lot of extra-curriculars. Things like the cinema or paid extracurriculars cost too much particularly for working class children. You have a lot of kids whose families have been financially squeezed out of being able to provide them a life, and wonder why they are angry and discotentful? Neo-Liberal hyper-individualism and me, me, me ideology means many people just cant look eye to eye anymore. You have an adult population that has also given up and is telling their kids that there is no point. 

In terms of disruptive behaviours? So many kids who aren't being raised by their parents now, particularly amongst younger Gen Z. 40% of kids entering school without even being f*cking potty trained. Kids have no accountability in school because their parents never moved past adolescent feelings of blaming everyone but themselves, which leads to a behaviour crisis in schools. 

However, the point is, that none of this is because of social media or people like Andrew Tate. They've been blaming social media since 2010 and surprise surprise, because the real issues aren't being addressed, the problem is getting worse. Until its admitted that this is all because of a systemic issue with our society and economic system, then it will only get worse. 

Blaming it on social media is just laziness and showing this slop in schools will only breed more resentment. The blame on the current failed political/economic system usually gets pushed aside to social media, however the mass proliferation of social media and mobile phones occurred around the same time the 2008 Recession hit, and since then wages have stagnated while bills have gone up, inflation has gone up, cost of living has nearly tripled, people say we recovered from 2008 but I don't believe it. The economy "grew" but our standard of living has collapsed. People need to face that we have been in a Depression since 2008.

​@diaryofshehtajsmommywe technically don't know, but it is heavily implied. We know he attacked her, and we can safely assume the attack ultimately led to her death. As in, he feels like he did what was the morally correct thing to do, rather than answering factually whether he did or did not commit murder.

So my verdict is that he is very likely guilty, but there still could be room for it being manslaughter rather than murder

comment_content: I don't know if it is fair, or even possible, to label a show, movie, or series as "perfect", but this is the closest thing that I have ever seen to that in 50+ years! It represents the exact definition of story telling. From the way it is filmed, to the acting, to the handling of the subject matter, it grabs your attention, it pulls you in, and it doesn't let you go. It is so thought-provoking, without telling you what to think. It begs for understanding, without providing any answers. It allows you to LIVE through what must be the scariest, most difficult thing a child, or a parent, or a police officer, or a clinical psychologist can experience. You as a viewer don't observe it, you experience it. It is a master class on how to properly tell a story in a visual medium. The cameras merely document what is happening, in the most immersive but unobtrusive way possible. (There are no "action" sequences simulated by a shaky cam. There are no emotions triggered by manipulative soundtracks). All of it, every ounce of feeling, is generated by expert acting, set design, production planning, and execution. I used to consider "Glengarry Glen Ross" a master class in acting. "Adolescence" is a master class in nearly every component that makes a movie or series. Obviously, there is nothing ever created that appeals to EVERYONE, but if you appreciate creativity, proper story telling, thoughtful dialog, and masterful acting, then you will remember this one for a long time. Oh, and I forgot to mention that they accomplished all of this while filming each of the 4 one-hour episodes IN ONE TAKE, while using multiple locations AND a child actor who is performing in his first role! It is heart-wrenching and mind-blowing. Well done!

reply_content:
@vibovitold I’ve watched the entire series, I’m not blind – and I stand by what I said. The show could’ve gone deeper by showing at least one scene with the parents’ reaction of the girl, or giving the girl a voice before her death. That perspective matters. Still, I give it a solid 8–9 because the acting was outstanding.

But let’s be honest: parents and the surrounding environment shape a child’s behavior more than people like to admit. When there’s emotional neglect, lack of communication, or even cruelty at home, it often leads to pain, confusion, and destructive choices. Not every case ends in murder – but many problems could be prevented if people paid more attention.

In most conflicts, it takes two sides. It’s rarely as simple as “victim and villain.” Everyone has a choice, yes – but those choices don’t happen in a vacuum. And when someone ends up dead, that’s not just tragic – it’s the worst possible outcome of many ignored warning signs.

If you don’t want to talk about that part of the story, fine – but don’t pretend it doesn’t exist.

comment_content: Sorry but the parents and the girl herself are also guilty for this tragedy. This is also the reason why this world is so shit! To focuse only on the boy his story. This series is very good mut sends bad message to the world. Like the bad behavier of the girl an others is more tolerated.. very bad
reply_content:
​@Panda-fn7hz 
"The show could’ve gone deeper by showing at least one scene with the parents’ reaction of the girl, or giving the girl a voice before her death. "

It could have done plenty of things (some people here complained there was no courtroom battle in it, they're so used to them in crime dramas).

I think this was a deliberate choice, to keep the story focused. 
Note that we watch how it unfolds mostly from the parents' perspective. 

(The one-shot technique is not a random gimmick - it confines us to "here and now", no jumping places, no retrospections, etc.)

And the parents most likely wouldn't really have had that girl on their radar, let alone before she died.

"But let’s be honest: parents and the surrounding environment shape a child’s behavior more than people like to admit."

Of course. And the parents are explicitly shown tormenting themselves about it.

The point is that the answer isn't always as obvious as "cruelty at home", or sexual abuse etc. 
These are cliches.

So once again it's a deliberate choice, in my personal opinion the right choice.
As Graham himself put it: "I wanted to eliminate the possibility of thinking Eddie is a violent man [...] I wanted Jamie’s mum not to be an alcoholic in any way, shape, or form".

And it corresponds with many real-life cases, where kids who murder someone don't come from troubled families.
There are some hints of how the father and the son weren't on the same wave (despite the dad meaning well), like in these football stories Jamie tells the psychologist, but these are very subtle. 

This story leaves many questions open, it's not supposed to be a parenting / crime prevention manual. 
That elevates it from a trivial cautionary tale. 
But that's a matter of taste.

comment_content:
Why did they misrepresent information in the show?

Incel is an actual real movement, with a very specific ideology, that exists in its purest form on forums on weird corners of the internet banned off most social media. And it is popular.
The real ones actually condone violence against women, and have led to incidents of murder, and mass murder in the past.
They have what’s called a black-pill philosophy.
Their ideology is not comparable with what Andrew Tate says. The belief system and worldview are completely different, there not even remotely comparable.

My point is, this doesn’t help the problems. It creates more confusion about what exactly it is that young people get wrapped up in.
*So it widens the vacuum, don’t you understand?*
I assume they researched this enough before making the show to understand this is the case yet did so anyway. 
You can still disagree with Tate, that’s fine, but it is objectively not an incel ideology.
This only strengthens their sense of persecution.
If you want to challenge these narratives, that’s fine, but why not do a proper job of it by actually bringing the true contents of these spaces into full view? The material needs to be demystified and clarified, and separated out into its different components.

Lies might have a temporary effect, politically, but they don’t last. 
The truth is the best weapon to have on your side. Face the content fairly, and you will win far more supporters in the long run, because people are not stupid and they know when someone’s coming from the right place.

Even when they refer to the “manosphere”, that’s not a movement. It’s more like a category, used to describe spaces where men speak. There are many different movements that would fall under the banner of manosphere, many of which are delineated by virtue of the fact they don’t agree with eachother. Many are non-toxic and positive.
So when the boy in the show says “it’s a call to action by the manosphere”, that doesn’t make any sense. It’s fear mongering, because it makes it sound militaristic, when really it’s a xenophobic fantasy.

There is genuine “call to action” by real incels, that has happened in the past and has led to mass murders.
Obfuscation never helps a problem.
Misinformation widens the vacuum, which leaves gaps for people to fall into.

​@schmelzikasefaust8193 the point of the show is not to be statistically representative.
the core idea on which the first act is based is that we can’t believe the angel-faced boy could have done it.
for some reason that i can’t quite put my finger on, that wouldn’t work as well if they tossed a rough 17-year-old black boy in the back of a police car.

similarly, "The Silence of the Lambs" obviously isn’t statistically representative, as most murder victims aren’t killed by elderly intellectuals - and something tells me it wasn’t the case in 1991, either.
such ageist propaganda!

i’ll go out on a limb and say that in both cases it was meant to create CONTRAST.
fiction isn’t under any obligation to be statistically representative. if it is, i must have missed that memo.

not to mention the show is not about stabbings in general.
it’s about a very specific, niche type of crime - incel-motivated.
and within this niche, black perpetrators seem to be rare - Elliot Rodger, Alek Minassian, the list goes on and i can’t recall a single (in)famous case where the killer was black.

if we talk about typical stabbings and killings on the street, there are already plenty of TV series that show that, also on Netflix, e.g. "Top Boy", where there are countless stabbings and street killings committed by gangs whose ethnic composition seems to align with reality.
